sourceMovie.title: Gandhi
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Gandhi (1982) as a drama theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The drama and history blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Darkest Hour
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Darkest Hour (2017) is the natural next step after Gandhi: same drama DNA, different perspective. The tense streak and history elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: The Last Emperor
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Gandhi who respond to its drama will find The Last Emperor (1987) immediately familiar. Both films share a history sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Malcolm X
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Gandhi fans keep landing on Malcolm X (1992) — the drama is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: The Boy Who Harnessed the Wind
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Boy Who Harnessed the Wind draws from the same drama well as Gandhi, channeling history in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2019 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: The Way Back
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Way Back (2010) captures the drama appeal of Gandhi without simply imitating it. The history streak running through both films is what puts The Way Back at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Suffragette
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama appeal of Gandhi — that tense edge, the history — translates cleanly into Suffragette (2015). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Escape from Pretoria
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Escape from Pretoria (2020) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps Gandhi rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Hunger
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Hunger (2008) is the kind of film Gandhi fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The drama overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: The Railway Man
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Railway Man shares Gandhi's gift for drama — the 2013 film delivers history without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Mandela: Long Walk to Freedom
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Gandhi compelling — the drama, the history — shows up in Mandela: Long Walk to Freedom (2013) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

faq.question: What should I watch after Gandhi?
faq.answer: Try Darkest Hour, The Last Emperor, Malcolm X—each shares DNA with Gandhi while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Gandhi?
faq.answer: Gandhi (1982) is best described as Drama, History. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Gandhi worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.6/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
